turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having some really bad stomach issues for a few weeks now. I'm getting diarrhea, like 8 to 10 times a day, and it's just not stopping, even after taking Imodium.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: The cramping is really bad, mostly on my left side. It's like a sharp stabbing pain that goes away for a second and then comes back again, making it hard to breathe when it hits.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: Yeah, I get really tired afterwards. It's like all my energy just gets drained out of me, and I feel like I'm running on fumes. It's been affecting my work too - I've had to take a few days off because I just couldn't keep up with the demands of my job.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: No, I don't really sweat that much when it happens. To be honest, I'm usually so focused on finding a bathroom and dealing with the pain that I don't even notice if I'm sweating or not.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: No, I don't really sweat that much when it happens. The main thing I'm worried about is getting to a bathroom quickly before things get too out of hand again.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The cramping seems to be mainly on my left side. Sometimes it feels like it's radiating down towards my pelvis, but for the most part, it stays pretty central, you know, right above where your appendix is, if that makes any sense.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient: No, it just feels like it's concentrated around my lower abdomen. I don't get any pain in my arms, neck, or jaw when these episodes happen.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, no tingling or numbness. To be honest, it feels like all my energy is going towards dealing with the pain and getting to a bathroom, so I don't really notice anything else happening in my body during those episodes.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: No, no tingling or numbness. My main focus is on dealing with the cramping and diarrhea, to be honest.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: No, I don't have any swelling in my ankles, feet, or hands. But I do have some achy pain in both knees and ankles, it's not severe but it's definitely there, and it doesn't seem to be related to the stomach issues or diarrhea, at least not directly.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: I'm not sure about my shoes feeling tighter, but I do have some achy pain in both knees and ankles. Like I said, it's not severe, but it's been going on for a while now.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient: The diarrhea is definitely upsetting my stomach, making me feel queasy. But to be honest, I'm so focused on getting to a bathroom and dealing with the pain that I don't really notice if I'm feeling nauseous or not. The main thing I'm aware of is the urgency and frequency of the bowel movements.

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: Yeah, it feels like my stomach is just churning nonstop, especially with the diarrhea. It's hard to explain, but it's like everything inside me is just moving around really fast and making it worse. And yeah, I'd say I feel queasy a lot of the time, especially after one of those episodes.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: I've actually had a low-grade fever a few times per week. It's not super high, usually around 37.8 to 38 degrees Celsius. I don't really feel like I'm burning up or anything, but it's definitely there. And yeah, sometimes I'll feel a bit clammy after an episode, but I wouldn't say I've had any full-blown night sweats.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient: To be honest, my appetite has been a bit off due to the stomach issues, and I haven't really felt like eating much of anything. But what's really concerning me is that I've lost about 4 kg (9 lbs) over the past three weeks, and it feels like I'm just wasting away from all this diarrhea and cramping.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient:
Honestly, my diet has been pretty irregular due to work. I've been grabbing takeout or eating on the go a lot of the time because I don't have much time for cooking. And when I do cook, it's usually something quick and easy like pasta or stir-fry. I haven't really been eating much fiber or anything that could be helping with my stomach issues.

As for exercise, I've actually been trying to get more into it lately since the weight loss has been a concern. But between work and dealing with these symptoms, it's hard to find the energy to go to the gym or do much of anything physical. I'm lucky if I can muster up the motivation to take a short walk around my apartment building.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient: To be honest, it's been really tough lately. I've always been pretty active, but since these symptoms started, I just don't have the energy for it. I used to go to the gym 3-4 times a week and take long walks on the weekends, but now even short walks around my apartment building feel like too much effort.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient: Honestly, it varies a lot. I usually try to grab something quick for breakfast, maybe some cereal or toast, and then I'm out the door for work. Lunch is often takeout from a nearby restaurant or something I can eat on the go. Dinners are also mostly takeout or leftovers if I'm lucky enough to have cooked something the night before. It's not great, but like I said, it's hard to find time to cook with my schedule.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My sister, she's actually been dealing with some health issues for a while now. She was diagnosed with Crohn's disease when she was 30 years old. That's been a bit of a concern for me, to be honest, since I'm experiencing similar symptoms and it seems like they've been going on for a pretty long time.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: Yes, my sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at 30 years old. It's been a concern for me because our symptoms seem similar and it's been going on for a pretty long time, so I'm worried that might be what's going on with me too.
